meta.purpose: Design reasoning for a Dutch sustainability assessment platform used by construction professionals and ecology consultants to evaluate environmental risk in construction projects.

INT-001.label: Risk Assessment Review
INT-001.description: Construction professionals need to immediately see overall project risk and drill into specific risk categories to make go/no-go decisions.
INT-001.principle: Risk visibility drives informed decision-making under time pressure
INT-001.implication: Overall risk score must be visible within 2 seconds of opening a project
INT-001.rule: Risk score is the first element in the visual hierarchy on every project page

INT-002.label: Project Comparison
INT-002.description: Compare risk profiles across multiple construction sites to prioritize which projects need immediate attention during weekly planning.
INT-002.principle: Comparison requires consistent visual encoding across items
INT-002.implication: All project cards must use identical layout, scale, and color mapping

INT-003.label: Report Generation
INT-003.description: Generate PDF reports conforming to Dutch environmental reporting standards for regulatory submission.
INT-003.principle: Regulatory output must be predictable and inspectable before submission
INT-003.implication: Preview report before generating; show exactly what will be submitted

INT-004.label: Field Data Entry
INT-004.description: Ecology consultants enter field survey data on tablets in outdoor conditions with potential gloved interaction.
INT-004.principle: Data entry in adverse conditions requires large targets and immediate validation
INT-004.implication: Touch targets minimum 48px, inline validation on every field

INT-005.label: Risk Category Deep Dive
INT-005.description: After seeing the overall risk, users drill into specific categories (soil, species, noise, water) to understand contributing factors and required actions.
INT-005.principle: Progressive disclosure from summary to detail reduces cognitive overload
INT-005.implication: Category pages show summary metrics first, expandable sections for detail

PSY-001.label: Cognitive Load Management
PSY-001.description: Construction professionals process complex multi-variable environmental data under time pressure. Cognitive load must be actively managed.
PSY-001.principle: Progressive disclosure reduces cognitive overload in data-dense environments
PSY-001.implication: Show summary first, details on demand. Never show all variables simultaneously.
PSY-001.anti_pattern: Showing all data fields on a single screen without hierarchy

PSY-002.label: Visual Hierarchy for Scanning
PSY-002.description: Users scan risk data in an F-pattern. Critical risk information must occupy the top-left quadrant and use size/color to stand out.
PSY-002.principle: Leverage natural F-pattern scanning for critical information placement
PSY-002.implication: Risk scores top-left, largest font size. Supporting data flows below and right.

PSY-003.label: Comparison Cognition
PSY-003.description: Comparing multiple risk profiles requires aligned visual dimensions. Misaligned scales or layouts break comparative judgment.
PSY-003.principle: Consistent encoding across compared items enables rapid judgment
PSY-003.implication: Project comparison cards must have identical structure, scale, and color mapping

PSY-004.label: Regulatory Confidence
PSY-004.description: Submitting regulatory reports is high-stakes. Users need to feel certain the output is correct and complete before submission.
PSY-004.principle: Uncertainty in high-stakes actions causes paralysis
PSY-004.implication: Show completeness checklist and preview before report generation

PSY-005.label: Error Prevention in Data Entry
PSY-005.description: Field data entry errors are costly to correct later. Prevent errors at input time with constraints and validation.
PSY-005.principle: Prevention is cheaper than correction in field-collected data
PSY-005.implication: Constrained inputs (dropdowns, ranges) over free text. Inline validation.

CUL-001.label: Dutch Directness
CUL-001.description: Dutch professional culture values direct, unambiguous communication. Risk labels should state the fact without hedging or euphemism.
CUL-001.principle: State risk directly — 'High risk' not 'Elevated concern level'
CUL-001.implication: Use plain Dutch risk terminology. No softening language.

CUL-002.label: Data-Dense Professional Tools
CUL-002.description: Dutch construction professionals expect information density similar to engineering tools like AutoCAD or Revit, not consumer app spaciousness.
CUL-002.principle: Information density signals professionalism and respect for expertise
CUL-002.implication: Compact spacing, smaller body text, dense tables. Whitespace is for structure, not decoration.

CUL-003.label: Dutch Regulatory Formality
CUL-003.description: Dutch environmental regulations (Wet natuurbescherming, EU directives) require precise, formal documentation. Reports must match expected bureaucratic format.
CUL-003.principle: Regulatory output format is non-negotiable and culturally prescribed
CUL-003.implication: Report layout follows Dutch regulatory conventions exactly

CUL-004.label: Color Semantics in Dutch Construction
CUL-004.description: In Dutch construction, red/yellow/green have specific regulatory weight beyond general UX conventions. Red means stop-work, not just 'attention needed'.
CUL-004.principle: Color semantics must match industry-specific regulatory meaning
CUL-004.implication: Red = stop/prohibited, Orange = action required, Yellow = caution, Green = proceed

EMO-001.label: Controlled Urgency
EMO-001.description: High-risk findings should create appropriate urgency without panic. The interface must project control even when delivering alarming data.
EMO-001.principle: Professional urgency through structure, not alarm
EMO-001.implication: Use color and prominence to signal severity, but maintain clean layout. No flashing, no exclamation marks.

EMO-002.label: Submission Confidence
EMO-002.description: Users submitting regulatory reports need to feel confident the data is complete and correct. Uncertainty leads to re-checking loops.
EMO-002.principle: Confidence comes from visible completeness, not reassuring language
EMO-002.implication: Show completion percentage, checklist of required sections, preview of final output

EMO-003.label: Field Work Frustration Prevention
EMO-003.description: Data entry in field conditions (rain, cold, gloves) is inherently frustrating. The interface must minimize friction, not add to it.
EMO-003.principle: Every unnecessary tap in field conditions is a frustration multiplier
EMO-003.implication: Minimize required taps. Auto-save. Large targets. Smart defaults.

AUD-001.label: Construction Professional
AUD-001.description: Site managers and project leads with 10-30 years experience. Highly technical, time-pressured, accustomed to data-dense engineering tools.

AUD-002.label: Ecology Consultant
AUD-002.description: Environmental scientists conducting field surveys and validating assessment outputs. Detail-oriented, comfortable with scientific data, works in field conditions.

VIS-001.label: Data-Dense Layout System
VIS-001.description: A compact, information-dense layout with minimal decorative whitespace, optimized for professional data consumption.
VIS-001.specification: max-width: 1400px; grid gap: 12px; padding: 16px; card padding: 12px 16px
VIS-001.reasoning: Construction professionals expect data density similar to engineering tools. Consumer-app spacing wastes valuable screen real estate and slows scanning.

VIS-002.label: Professional Typography Scale
VIS-002.description: A compact typography scale with clear hierarchy, optimized for data scanning rather than reading comfort.
VIS-002.specification: risk-score: 32px/700; section-header: 16px/600; body: 14px/400; data-label: 12px/500; data-value: 14px/600. Font: system-ui.
VIS-002.reasoning: Smaller body text than consumer apps, but clear hierarchy via weight and size differentiation. System font for reliability.

VIS-003.label: Risk Severity Color System
VIS-003.description: A four-tier color system matching Dutch construction regulatory conventions for risk communication.
VIS-003.specification: critical/stop: #DC2626; action-required: #EA580C; caution: #CA8A04; safe/proceed: #16A34A. Each with icon + text label.
VIS-003.reasoning: Colors match Dutch construction industry conventions where red means stop-work order, not just attention. Always paired with icon and text for accessibility.

VIS-004.label: Risk Communication Microcopy
VIS-004.description: Direct, unhedged Dutch-language risk labels and action descriptions matching professional expectations.
VIS-004.specification: Risk levels: 'Hoog risico', 'Actie vereist', 'Let op', 'Veilig'. Actions: imperative form, max 8 words.
VIS-004.reasoning: Dutch directness requires stating risk plainly. Hedging language ('slightly elevated') undermines trust and delays action.

VIS-005.label: Progressive Disclosure Pattern
VIS-005.description: Summary-to-detail drill-down pattern: overview cards with key metrics, expandable to full category detail.
VIS-005.specification: Level 1: risk score + category name + trend arrow. Level 2: expand to metrics table + contributing factors. Level 3: link to full category page.
VIS-005.reasoning: Three-level disclosure matches the mental model of construction professionals: quick scan, evaluate, then deep dive only if needed.

VIS-006.label: Comparison Grid Layout
VIS-006.description: Horizontally aligned project cards with consistent structure for side-by-side risk comparison.
VIS-006.specification: grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fill, minmax(280px, 1fr)); consistent card height via min-height; identical internal layout per card
VIS-006.reasoning: Comparison requires visual alignment. Cards with different layouts break the user's ability to compare at a glance.

VIS-007.label: Report Preview and Confidence UI
VIS-007.description: Pre-submission UI showing completeness checklist, preview of the exact output, and clear submit action.
VIS-007.specification: Completeness indicator: segmented progress bar. Preview: paginated PDF-like view. Submit: primary button, disabled until 100% complete.
VIS-007.reasoning: Regulatory submission is high-stakes. Users need visible proof of completeness and an exact preview to submit with confidence.

VIS-008.label: Field-Optimized Input Controls
VIS-008.description: Large, constrained input controls optimized for tablet use in outdoor conditions with potential gloved interaction.
VIS-008.specification: min touch target: 48px; input height: 48px; dropdown over free text; inline validation; auto-save on blur; high-contrast borders
VIS-008.reasoning: Field conditions make precise touch difficult. Large targets, constrained inputs, and auto-save prevent data loss and reduce frustration.

A11Y-001.label: Color-Independent Risk Communication
A11Y-001.description: Risk levels must be communicated through icon shape, text label, and color together. Color alone is never sufficient.
A11Y-001.specification: Every risk level: unique icon shape + text label + color. Icons: octagon (stop), triangle (warning), circle-info (caution), checkmark (safe).
A11Y-001.rule: Never use color as the sole indicator of risk severity

A11Y-002.label: WCAG AA Contrast in Dense Layout
A11Y-002.description: All text must meet WCAG 2.1 AA contrast ratios even in the compact, data-dense layout with small font sizes.
A11Y-002.specification: Normal text (< 18px): 4.5:1 minimum. Large text (>= 18px bold): 3:1 minimum. UI components: 3:1 against adjacent colors.
A11Y-002.rule: Test all text/background combinations at the smallest font size used (12px data labels)

A11Y-003.label: Touch Target Sizing for Field Use
A11Y-003.description: All interactive elements must meet minimum touch target sizes for use with gloves and in challenging field conditions.
A11Y-003.specification: Minimum touch target: 48x48px with 8px gap between targets. Buttons: min-height 48px. Form inputs: min-height 48px.
A11Y-003.rule: No interactive element may be smaller than 48x48px in field-facing views

A11Y-004.label: Screen Reader Support for Reports
A11Y-004.description: Report pages must be fully navigable by screen reader with proper heading hierarchy, landmark regions, and data table markup.
A11Y-004.specification: Semantic HTML: h1-h3 hierarchy, nav/main/aside landmarks, caption + th scope on data tables, aria-live for dynamic risk updates.
A11Y-004.rule: All report pages must pass axe-core automated testing with zero violations

reasoning_chains.name: Risk Assessment to Visual Hierarchy
reasoning_chains.description: From the core risk assessment intent through visual scanning psychology and Dutch data density expectations to a compact, accessible layout with professional typography.

reasoning_chains.name: Risk Communication to Color System
reasoning_chains.description: From risk assessment through Dutch construction color conventions and directness culture to a four-tier color system with icon and text redundancy for accessibility.

reasoning_chains.name: Risk Drill-Down to Progressive Disclosure
reasoning_chains.description: From the category deep-dive intent through cognitive load management to a three-level progressive disclosure pattern with screen reader support.

reasoning_chains.name: Project Comparison to Grid Layout
reasoning_chains.description: From project comparison needs through comparison cognition and data density culture to a consistent, accessible comparison grid.

reasoning_chains.name: Report Generation to Confidence UI
reasoning_chains.description: From report generation through regulatory confidence psychology and Dutch formality norms to a completeness-checklist UI with preview.

reasoning_chains.name: Field Data Entry to Optimized Controls
reasoning_chains.description: From field data entry through error prevention psychology and field frustration to large, constrained, auto-saving input controls with adequate touch targets.

reasoning_chains.name: Dutch Directness to Risk Microcopy
reasoning_chains.description: From risk assessment through Dutch directness culture and controlled urgency to plain, unhedged Dutch risk labels.

reasoning_chains.name: Audience Expertise to Data Density
reasoning_chains.description: From construction professional expertise through cultural expectations of data density to compact layout optimized for expert users.

ANTI-001.label: Generic SaaS Dashboard
ANTI-001.description: Using a standard SaaS dashboard grid with large cards, excessive whitespace, customizable widgets, and drag-and-drop layout.
ANTI-001.why_prohibited: Construction professionals need task-oriented, data-dense interfaces. Generic dashboards waste screen space, slow down risk scanning, and signal consumer-grade tooling that undermines professional trust.

ANTI-002.label: Decorative Animations
ANTI-002.description: Using transitions, loading animations, or micro-interactions that serve aesthetic rather than functional purposes.
ANTI-002.why_prohibited: Every animation adds latency to safety-critical risk data. Construction professionals interpret delays as unreliability. Functional feedback (progress bars, spinners during load) is fine; decorative motion is waste.

ANTI-003.label: Softened Risk Language
ANTI-003.description: Using hedging or softening language for risk levels: 'slightly elevated', 'area of concern', 'potential issue'.
ANTI-003.why_prohibited: Dutch construction culture demands directness. Softened language delays action, creates ambiguity in safety-critical decisions, and undermines user trust in the platform's authority.

ANTI-004.label: Color-Only Risk Indication
ANTI-004.description: Using color as the sole differentiator between risk levels without icon or text redundancy.
ANTI-004.why_prohibited: Violates WCAG accessibility requirements. Approximately 8% of men have color vision deficiency. Risk indication must use color + icon shape + text label together.

ANTI-005.label: Onboarding Wizards and Tutorials
ANTI-005.description: Adding step-by-step onboarding flows, feature tours, or tutorial overlays.
ANTI-005.why_prohibited: Expert users with 10-30 years experience find onboarding patronizing. The interface should be self-evident to domain experts. A professional tool teaches through use, not through wizards.

ANTI-006.label: Small Touch Targets in Field Views
ANTI-006.description: Using standard web input sizes (32px height, 4px spacing) for field-facing data entry views.
ANTI-006.why_prohibited: Field conditions (gloves, rain, shaking) make precise touch impossible. Undersized targets cause input errors that are expensive to correct after the field visit.

process: 1. Identify which user intent the component serves (INT-001 through INT-005)
2. Use resolve_for_component or traverse from that intent to find visual properties
3. Check the component description against anti-patterns using check_anti_patterns
4. Apply the resolved visual properties: layout (VIS-001/006), typography (VIS-002), colors (VIS-003), content (VIS-004), interaction patterns (VIS-005/007/008)
5. Ensure accessibility nodes are respected: color independence (A11Y-001), contrast (A11Y-002), touch targets (A11Y-003), screen reader (A11Y-004)
6. Use Dutch-language labels where the spec indicates (VIS-004 linguistic_examples)

example_traversal.component: Risk score card on project overview

resolved_properties.layout: Dense layout (VIS-001): 12px grid gap, 12px/16px card padding
resolved_properties.typography: Risk score at 32px/700, category headers at 16px/600 (VIS-002)
resolved_properties.colors: Four-tier risk colors with icon + text (VIS-003)
